name: Financial Ledger
description: SCOPE AND ROOT PROBLEM. LEDGER is Tally's provider-neutral financial system of record. Dirk needs exact personal-spend actuals across three owned accounts at two banks, but one economic event may first arrive through a daily notification and later reappear on a monthly statement. A single provenance field or statement-import-created row cannot prove whether these observations are the same event. Budget reporting also fails if bank account, payment instrument, cardholder, spend pool, and category are treated as one dimension. The root problem is therefore broader than transaction storage: Tally needs one canonical event identity with multiple privacy-conscious evidence records, attributable reconciliation history, and independent financial attribution so later modules can trust exact actuals.

SELECTED APPROACH AND GOVERNING BOUNDARY. Retain the local-first, single-process Tally CLI and embedded store. ADR-CORE-0030 and ARCH-CORE-TALLY-SYSTEM-CONTEXT govern the corrected boundary: Hermes is an external orchestrator that owns AgentMail, WhatsApp, provider payloads, clarification, schedules, recipients, rendering, delivery, and retries. LEDGER owns financial validation, canonical facts, generic evidence metadata and opaque references, reconciliation decisions, attribution, exact actuals, idempotency, and audit history. CHANNELS is not a Ledger implementation dependency. Existing Actions CORE artifacts remain prior art only; FastEndpoints, Aspire, PostgreSQL, RLS, multi-tenancy, and browser assumptions are not inherited.

ACTORS AND CURRENT STATE. P1 Personal Finance Owner remains the sole human actor and decision-maker. Hermes or another External Orchestrator is a supporting automated client, not a persona and not an independent financial principal. Today the owner inspects separate notifications and statements, manually decides whether observations describe the same transaction, infers account or cardholder context, removes suspected transfers or duplicates, assigns categories and funding responsibility, and recalculates totals. Pain points are delayed work, false or missed matches, one-source provenance, provider payload privacy, dimension conflation, and no deterministic proof that a statement reconciles to the already-recorded ledger.

DESIRED WORKFLOWS AND GAP. Existing account, transaction, category, transfer/refund, correction, query, contract, backup, and migration workflows remain. UC-LEDGER-002 now records one transaction with initial generic evidence; UC-LEDGER-005 queries evidence/reconciliation and pool dimensions; UC-LEDGER-006 is the provider-neutral external-orchestrator contract; UC-LEDGER-009 and UC-LEDGER-010 compose correction/refund behavior with evidence and pools. New workflows add evidence (UC-LEDGER-013), deterministic or statement-only reconciliation (UC-LEDGER-014), explicit owner resolution and correction (UC-LEDGER-015), completed-statement coverage exceptions (UC-LEDGER-016), payment-instrument/cardholder attribution (UC-LEDGER-017), and spend-pool maintenance/assignment (UC-LEDGER-018). File parsing and batch orchestration remain INGEST concerns; planned amounts remain BUDGET; analytical reports remain INSIGHTS; provider interaction remains Hermes.

INTEGRATED DOMAIN RULES. One economic event has at most one active Canonical Transaction even when several Evidence Records support it. Evidence kinds are provider-neutral: agent capture, statement row, receipt, external document, and owner assertion. Ledger retains only stable evidence identity, privacy-safe metadata, fingerprints when needed, opaque external references, links, and decisions; it never stores or interprets mailbox, MIME, messaging, provider-cursor, credential, recipient, or delivery payloads. Evidence registration and linking are idempotent. One transaction may have many evidence records, while one active evidence record cannot confirm more than one canonical transaction.

Reconciliation is append-only and attributable. Observable outcomes include recorded unreconciled, statement reconciled, statement-only, recorded absent from an approved statement scope, ambiguous match, owner-confirmed match, and reconciliation exception. Automatic application requires a documented deterministic policy that yields exactly one compatible active candidate. Zero candidates may create one statement-only transaction only through an approved INGEST request. Multiple or conflicting candidates remain unapplied. Owner confirmation, rejection, revocation, and replacement require explicit intent and reason. An absent-from-statement result is valid only for a complete approved account/period scope; incomplete scopes cannot imply absence.

Account, Payment Instrument, Cardholder Attribution, Spend Pool, and Spend Category are separate stable dimensions. LEDGER never infers one solely from another or from provider wording. Hermes may map wording to stable Tally identifiers, but Ledger validates those identifiers and records attributable history. Unknown instrument/cardholder attribution remains explicit. Each spend transaction initially has at most one active Pool Assignment or appears in an unassigned-pool bucket; A15 and OQ-LEDGER-15 preserve this as a validation gate rather than fact. At minimum the owner can represent company-paid discretionary or personal-expense spending separately from personal after-tax spending.

Exactness composes across dimensions. Transfers contribute zero External Spend to every pool. A linked refund or reversal uses its own Effective Date and offsets the original transaction's current category and current pool. Category or pool corrections, relationship changes, voids, and supersessions update active actuals atomically while retaining history. Void or supersession never silently moves evidence, a reconciliation confirmation, category, pool, instrument/cardholder attribution, transfer, or refund relationship to a replacement. Affected reconciliation state becomes an explicit unresolved or exception outcome until a new valid decision exists.

PRE-MORTEM. Six months after release, the most damaging failures are: a weak match hides a duplicate as authoritative (RISK-LEDGER-008); account/instrument/cardholder/pool/category conflation yields plausible but wrong actuals (RISK-LEDGER-009); evidence metadata leaks provider or financial payloads (RISK-LEDGER-010); duplicate, missing, or incorrectly normalized facts corrupt every downstream result (RISK-LEDGER-001); or contract/storage drift prevents safe recovery (RISK-LEDGER-004/007). These drive deterministic-only matching, explicit ambiguity, independent dimensions, payload-free evidence, global idempotency, and complete durable-state recovery.
DOMAIN AREA DISPOSITIONS.

- auth/authz — CHECK: one trusted local owner OS identity remains the authority; Hermes receives delegated invocation capability but no independent principal or bypass. Any hosted or multi-person operation invalidates this boundary.

- data model and persistence — DEEP: canonical facts, multiple evidence records, reconciliation decisions/scopes/exceptions, attribution histories, pools, lifecycle, relationships, idempotency, snapshots, backups, and migration are durable correctness state.
- UI and navigation — N/A: LEDGER has no frontend or conversation. Hermes or another external orchestrator owns every human-facing surface; CHANNELS is not an implementation module.

- integrations — DEEP: the External Orchestrator consumes the public contract; INGEST submits approved statement evidence and decisions; CLASSIFY applies only through public Ledger operations; BUDGET and INSIGHTS consume exact projections. AgentMail, WhatsApp, and bank providers are outside LEDGER.
- background and async — N/A: LEDGER runs on explicit invocation with no required daemon, watcher, polling, schedule, or delivery retry. Hermes owns schedules and provider lifecycle.

- reporting and analytics — CHECK: LEDGER returns exact snapshot membership and totals by pool/category/evidence/reconciliation dimensions. Planned amounts and analytical narrative remain BUDGET and INSIGHTS.
- notifications — N/A: no Ledger operation sends, acknowledges, schedules, throttles, or retries a notification; it returns structured results only.
- configuration and administration — DEEP: the owner maintains stable accounts, instruments, cardholders, spend pools, and categories plus attributable assignment/correction/archive history.
- multi-tenancy and isolation — N/A: one local owner and one writer remain in scope; multi-user roles, tenancy, and hosted synchronization require new decisions.
- financial semantics — DEEP: exact money/date/sign, transfers, refunds, lifecycle, category and pool conservation determine whether actuals are trustworthy.
- evidence and reconciliation — DEEP: multi-evidence identity, deterministic matching, owner confirmation, coverage scope, ambiguity, correction, and replay are the principal new requirements.

- local security and recovery — DEEP: evidence and attribution expand sensitive durable state; owner-only artifacts, payload-free diagnostics, host protection, verified backup/restore, and atomic migration remain mandatory.

- compliance — CHECK: the current sole-owner household activity remains within the recorded personal/household POPIA reasoning. Hosted, commercial, multi-person, or third-party financial processing requires reassessment.

SECURITY AND PRIVACY. Selective threat analysis focuses on disclosure, tampering, repudiation, false reconciliation, duplicate replay, and loss. Raw provider payloads and secrets remain with Hermes. Ledger stores minimal generic evidence, uses opaque references without provider interpretation, validates explicit owner authority for judgment decisions, preserves append-only history, emits payload-free diagnostics, and includes every new state type in restricted backup and recovery verification. The existing host-encryption and no-custom-cryptography decision remains valid.

FEASIBILITY. G1-G8 remain feasible under the prior discovery and storage spike. G9 is known/high confidence using existing immutable-history and idempotency discipline. G10 is complex/medium confidence: the lifecycle is feasible, but the automatic-match subset requires private empirical validation through OQ-LEDGER-13 and A14 before design hardens a policy. G11 is known/medium confidence: stable optional attribution is feasible, but initial privacy-safe identifiers require OQ-LEDGER-14 validation. G12 is known/medium-high confidence: first-class pool identities and exact totals are feasible, while one-active-pool cardinality remains A15/OQ-LEDGER-15. None of these questions blocks PRD authoring because the PRD can require explicit ambiguity, unknown attribution, unassigned pools, and no guessing; they gate later detailed design.

KILL-CRITERIA RESULT. No monitored criterion is triggered. The new scope does not require online-banking credentials, a hosted service, a daemon, or custom cryptography. A thin existing-finance-CLI adapter is less suitable now because it would also need Tally's provider-neutral multi-evidence lineage, append-only reconciliation decisions, independent attribution dimensions, uniform structured contract, and verified recovery semantics. Exact pool/category conservation and one-transaction-per-event remain provable requirements; inability to design them would trigger the existing correctness criterion later.

DISCOVERY HANDOFF. The next PRD revision must formalize G9-G12, UC-LEDGER-013 through UC-LEDGER-018, C18-C23, NG9, A14-A15, OQ-LEDGER-13 through OQ-LEDGER-15, RISK-LEDGER-008 through RISK-LEDGER-010, and the three new success metrics. It must revise prior provenance, transaction, query, correction, recovery, external-client, and CHANNELS language rather than layering contradictory requirements. Existing technical design and PLAN-LEDGER-V1 are intentionally stale until the revised PRD and design are reviewed.
problem_statement: Dirk cannot trust personal-spend actuals across three accounts at two banks when one economic event may be captured from a daily notification and later appear again on a monthly statement, evidence cannot be linked or reconciled without duplication, and account, payment instrument, cardholder, spend pool, and category are conflated. Root cause: Tally lacks a provider-neutral canonical ledger with multi-evidence lineage, attributable reconciliation decisions, and independent attribution dimensions. Desired journey: the owner or Hermes records one event with generic evidence, INGEST later reconciles statement evidence match-first, ambiguous or absent cases remain reviewable, the owner corrects attribution explicitly, and BUDGET or INSIGHTS consumes exact pool/category results through public contracts.
why_now: The existing plans assume one provenance source, statement imports that create transactions, and category-only actuals. Reconciliation and spend-pool ownership must be formalized before implementation hardens those assumptions and before notification and statement histories accumulate duplicate or misattributed events.
